title: AllMusic

infobox.value: [Online database](/wiki/List_of_online_music_databases "List of online music databases") for music albums, artists and songs; reviews and biographies

(previously known as **All-Music Guide** and **AMG**) is an American [online music database](/wiki/Online_database "Online database"). It catalogs more than three million [album](/wiki/Album "Album") entries and 30 million tracks, as well as information on [musicians](/wiki/Musical_artist "Musical artist") and [bands](/wiki/Musical_ensemble "Musical ensemble"). Initiated in 1991, the database was first made available on the Internet in 1994.[[2]](#cite_note-Wired-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Metro-3) AllMusic has been owned by [RhythmOne](/wiki/RhythmOne "RhythmOne") since 2015.

Erlewine, Michael; Bultman, Scott (1992). *All Music Guide: The Best Cds, Albums & Tapes : The Experts' Guide to the Best Releases from Thousands of Artists in All Types of Music*. Backbeat Books. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-87930-264-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-87930-264-1 "Special:BookSources/978-0-87930-264-1"). [OCLC](/wiki/OCLC_(identifier) "OCLC (identifier)") [27307106](https://search.worldcat.org/oclc/27307106).

Nosowitz, Dan (January 30, 2015). ["The Story of AllMusic, Which Predates the World Wide Web"](https://www.vice.com/en/article/the-internets-most-complete-guide-to-music/). *[Vice](/wiki/Vice_(magazine) "Vice (magazine)")*. Retrieved June 22, 2017.
Smith, Ernie (September 20, 2016). ["The Big Data Jukebox"](https://tedium.co/2016/09/20/allmusic-database-historic-importance/). *tedium.com*. Tedium. Retrieved July 26, 2017.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American online music database

This article is about the online database. For the album by Warne Marsh, see [All Music (album)](/wiki/All_Music_(album) "All Music (album)").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(July 2024)* |

AllMusic

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Logotype](/wiki/Logotype "Logotype") and [wordmark](/wiki/Wordmark "Wordmark") since July 2013 | |
| Type of site | [Online database](/wiki/List_of_online_music_databases "List of online music databases") for music albums, artists and songs; reviews and biographies |
| Available in | English |
| Owner | [RhythmOne](/wiki/RhythmOne "RhythmOne") (since 2015)[[1]](#cite_note-rhythmone-1) |
| Created by | [Michael Erlewine](/wiki/Michael_Erlewine "Michael Erlewine") |
| URL | [allmusic.com](https://www.allmusic.com/) |
| Commercial | Yes |
| Registration | Optional |
| Launched | 1991; 35 years ago (1991) (as All Music Guide) |
| Current status | [Online](/wiki/Online "Online") |

**AllMusic** (previously known as **All-Music Guide** and **AMG**) is an American [online music database](/wiki/Online_database "Online database"). It catalogs more than three million [album](/wiki/Album "Album") entries and 30 million tracks, as well as information on [musicians](/wiki/Musical_artist "Musical artist") and [bands](/wiki/Musical_ensemble "Musical ensemble"). Initiated in 1991, the database was first made available on the Internet in 1994.[[2]](#cite_note-Wired-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Metro-3) AllMusic has been owned by [RhythmOne](/wiki/RhythmOne "RhythmOne") since 2015.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AllMusic&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

AllMusic was launched as *All-Music Guide* by [Michael Erlewine](/wiki/Michael_Erlewine "Michael Erlewine"), a "compulsive archivist, noted astrologer, Buddhist scholar, and musician". He became interested in using computers for his astrological work in the mid-1970s and founded a software company, Matrix, in 1977. In the early 1990s, as [compact discs](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") (CDs) replaced [LPs](/wiki/LP_record "LP record") and [cassettes](/wiki/Cassette_(format) "Cassette (format)") as the dominant format for recorded music, Erlewine purchased what he thought was a CD of early recordings by [Little Richard](/wiki/Little_Richard "Little Richard"). After buying it, he discovered it was a "flaccid latter-day rehash".[[3]](#cite_note-Metro-3) Frustrated with the labeling, he researched using [metadata](/wiki/Metadata "Metadata") to create a music guide.[[4]](#cite_note-Videoland_book-4) In 1990, in [Big Rapids, Michigan](/wiki/Big_Rapids,_Michigan "Big Rapids, Michigan"), he founded *All Music Guide* with a goal to create an open-access database that included every recording "since [Enrico Caruso](/wiki/Enrico_Caruso "Enrico Caruso") gave the industry its first big boost".[[2]](#cite_note-Wired-2)

The first *All Music Guide*, published in 1992, was a 1,200-page reference book, packaged with a CD-ROM, titled *All Music Guide: The Best CDs, Albums & Tapes: The Expert's Guide to the Best Releases from Thousands of Artists in All Types of Music*.[[5]](#cite_note-AllMusic1stEdition-5) Its first online version, in 1994, was a text-based [Gopher](/wiki/Gopher_(protocol) "Gopher (protocol)") site.[[2]](#cite_note-Wired-2)[[6]](#cite_note-Vice_history-6) It moved to the [World Wide Web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web") as [web browsers](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser") became more user-friendly.[[3]](#cite_note-Metro-3)

Erlewine hired a database engineer, [Vladimir Bogdanov](/wiki/Vladimir_Bogdanov_(editor) "Vladimir Bogdanov (editor)"), to design the *All Music Guide* framework, and recruited his nephew, writer [Stephen Thomas Erlewine](/wiki/Stephen_Thomas_Erlewine "Stephen Thomas Erlewine"), to develop editorial content. In 1993, Chris Woodstra joined the staff as an engineer. A "record geek" who had written for alternative weeklies and fanzines, his main qualification was an "encyclopedic knowledge of music".[[3]](#cite_note-Metro-3) AllMusic developed a list of 1,400 subgenres of music, a feature that became central to the site's utility. In a 2016 article in *Tedium*, Ernie Smith wrote: "AllMusic may have been one of the most ambitious sites of the early-internet era—and it's one that is fundamental to our understanding of pop culture. Because the thing is, it doesn't just track reviews or albums. It tracks styles, genres, and subgenres, along with the tone of the music and the platforms on which the music is sold. It then connects that data together, in a way that can intelligently tell you about an entire type of music, whether a massive genre like classical, or a tiny one like [sadcore](/wiki/Sadcore "Sadcore")."[[7]](#cite_note-big_data_smith-7)

In 1996, seeking to further develop its web-based businesses, Alliance Entertainment Corp. bought All Music from Erlewine for a reported $3.5 million. He left the company after its sale.[[3]](#cite_note-Metro-3) Alliance filed for bankruptcy in 1999, and its assets were acquired by [Ron Burkle](/wiki/Ron_Burkle "Ron Burkle")'s Yucaipa Equity Fund.[[4]](#cite_note-Videoland_book-4)

In 1999, All Music relocated from Big Rapids to [Ann Arbor, Michigan](/wiki/Ann_Arbor,_Michigan "Ann Arbor, Michigan"), where the staff expanded from 12 to 100 people.[[3]](#cite_note-Metro-3) By February of that year, 350,000 albums and two million tracks had been cataloged. All Music had published biographies of 30,000 artists, 120,000 record reviews, and 300 essays written by "a hybrid of historians, critics, and passionate collectors".[[8]](#cite_note-Weisbard_Voice-8)[[9]](#cite_note-NPR-9)

In late 2007, AllMusic was purchased for $72 million by [TiVo Corporation](/wiki/TiVo_Corporation "TiVo Corporation") (known as Macrovision at the time of the sale, and as Rovi from 2009 until 2016).[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 2012, AllMusic removed all of [Bryan Adams](/wiki/Bryan_Adams "Bryan Adams")' info from the site per a request from the artist.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 2015, AllMusic was purchased by BlinkX, later known as [RhythmOne](/wiki/RhythmOne "RhythmOne").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[1]](#cite_note-rhythmone-1) The AllMusic database is powered by a combination of [MySQL](/wiki/MySQL "MySQL") and [MongoDB](/wiki/MongoDB "MongoDB").[[13]](#cite_note-ernie_smith_vice-13)

## The All Music Guide series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AllMusic&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The All Music Guide series")]

The All Media Network produced the *All Music Guide: The Definitive Guide* (at first released as *The Experts' Guide*),[[3]](#cite_note-Metro-3) which includes a series of publications about various music genres. It was followed by the *Required Listening* series, and Annual guides. [Vladimir Bogdanov](/wiki/Vladimir_Bogdanov_(editor) "Vladimir Bogdanov (editor)") is the president and the main editor of the series.[[14]](#cite_note-Billboard-14)

* *All Music Guide: The Definitive Guide to Popular Music* (1st edition: 1992, 2nd ed: 1994, 3rd ed: 1997, 4th ed: 2001, 5th ed: 2008)
* *All Music Guide to Classical Music: The Definitive Guide to Classical Music* (2004)
* *All Music Guide to Country: The Definitive Guide to Country Music* (1st ed: 1997, 2nd ed: 2003)
* *All Music Guide to Electronica: The Definitive Guide to Electronic Music* (2001)
* *All Music Guide to Hip-hop: The Definitive Guide to Rap & Hip-hop* (2003)
* *[All Music Guide to Jazz: The Definitive Guide to Jazz Music](/wiki/All_Music_Guide_to_Jazz "All Music Guide to Jazz")*  (1st ed: 1994, 2nd ed: 1996, 3rd ed: 1998, 4th ed: 2002)
* *All Music Guide to Rock: The Definitive Guide to Rock, Pop, and Soul* (1st ed: 1995, 2nd ed: 1997, 3rd ed: 2002)[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* *All Music Guide to Soul: The Definitive Guide to R&B and Soul* (2003)
* *[All Music Guide to the Blues: The Definitive Guide to the Blues](/wiki/All_Music_Guide_to_the_Blues "All Music Guide to the Blues")* (1st ed: 1996, 2nd ed: 1999, 3rd ed: 2003)
* *All Music Guide Required Listening: Classic Rock* (2007)
* *All Music Guide Required Listening: Contemporary Country* (2008)
* *All Music Guide Required Listening: Old School Rap & Hip-hop* (2008)
* *All Music Guide to the Music of 2002: Your Guide to the Recordings of the Year* (2003)
* *All Music Guide to the Music of 2003: Your Guide to the Recordings of the Year* (2004)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AllMusic&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Reception")]

In August 2007, *[PC Magazine](/wiki/PCMag "PCMag")* included AllMusic in its "Top 100 Classic Websites" list.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[2]](#cite_note-Wired-2)
